items.question: By 2030, I ___ my degree.
explanations: 'Will finish' is a single future action; 'by 2030' means completed before that point.
Correct! Completed before a future point takes the future perfect: 'will have finished'.
'Finish' is present simple, not this completed-by-2030 meaning.
'Finished' is past simple, but 2030 is in the future.

items.question: By June, they ___ married.
explanations: Correct! Future perfect = will have + participle: 'will have got married' (get → got).
After 'will have' you need the participle 'got', not the base 'get'.
'Will got' is missing 'have'. Use 'will have got'.
'Get' is present simple, but this is completed by June.

items.question: By the time you arrive, we ___ dinner.
explanations: 'Will eat' is a single action; 'by the time' means completed before you arrive.
Correct! 'By the time' + a completed action takes the future perfect: 'will have eaten'.
'Eat' is present simple, not this completed action.
'Ate' is past simple, but this is about the future.

items.question: Don't worry — she ___ left by then.
explanations: Correct! Negative future perfect = won't have + participle: 'won't have left'.
'Won't left' is missing 'have'. Use 'won't have left'.
'Doesn't have' is present simple, not the future perfect.
'Isn't left' is not correct here; use 'won't have left'.

items.question: By ten, I ___ the report (it'll be completely done).
explanations: 'Will be finishing' means in progress at ten, but the report will be fully done.
Correct! Completely done by a future point takes the future perfect: 'will have finished'.
'Finish' is present simple, not this completed-by-ten meaning.
'Finished' is past simple, but ten o'clock is in the future.

items.question: By 3pm, the plane ___.
explanations: 'Will land' is a single action; 'by 3pm' means completed before then.
Correct! Completed before a future point takes the future perfect: 'will have landed'.
'Lands' is present simple (a timetable), not this completed-by-3pm meaning.
'Landed' is past simple, but 3pm is in the future.

items.question: ___ you ___ everything by Friday?
explanations: Correct! Future perfect question = Will + subject + have + participle: 'Will you have finished…?'.
'Do you finish' asks about a habit, not completion by Friday.
'Will you finished' is missing 'have'. Use 'Will you have finished?'.
'Are you finish' mixes structures. Use 'Will you have finished?'.

items.question: By midnight, he ___ home.
explanations: 'Went' is the past simple; after 'will have' you need the participle 'gone'.
Correct! 'go → went → gone', so 'will have gone'.
'Will gone' is missing 'have'. Use 'will have gone'.
'Goes' is present simple, but this is completed by midnight.

items.question: Rewrite with the same meaning: 'I'll finish the book before Sunday.'
options: By Sunday, I'll have finished the book.
On Sunday I finish the book.
I finished the book on Sunday.
I'll be finishing the book on Sunday.
explanations: Correct! 'Finish before Sunday' = completed by Sunday = future perfect: 'will have finished'.
'On Sunday I finish' is a present-simple habit or timetable, not completion before Sunday.
'I finished' is past simple, but this is about the future.
'Will be finishing on Sunday' means in progress on Sunday, not completed before it.

items.question: Which sentence means the action is complete by that time?
options: At six I'll be cooking dinner.
By six I'll have cooked dinner.
explanations: 'Will be cooking' means in progress at six, not complete.
Correct! 'By six I'll have cooked' means the cooking is finished by six.
'I cook dinner' is a present habit.
'I'll cook at six' is a single future action, not completion by six.
